one_line_takeaway: A modular Selector/Mutator/Constraint/Evaluator framework that unifies 11 published LLM jailbreak methods for fair benchmarking, revealing a ~63% average breach rate across 10 models — including 57% on GPT-3.5-Turbo and 33% on GPT-4.
key_finding: Contemporary LLMs are pervasively vulnerable: across the 10 models the average breach probability was 63% (reported as ~60% in the abstract/conclusion). Even frontier models were not immune — GPT-3.5-Turbo averaged 57% ASR and GPT-4-0613 averaged 33% ASR. Closed-source models (GPT-3.5/GPT-4) averaged 45% ASR versus 66% for open-source models, though the Llama2 series was an exception with security comparable to GPT-4 (Llama2-7B 31%, Llama2-13B 37%). Larger size did not improve security: 13B variants of both Llama2 and Vicuna were slightly MORE vulnerable than their 7B counterparts. By method, long-tail encoding attacks were most effective on average (CodeChameleon 80%, JailBroken 76%, MultiLingual 76%, AutoDAN 75%, Cipher 74%), while ICA (32%) and DeepInception (32%) were weakest. For evaluators, GPT4-turbo judging was most accurate (93.5% accuracy, 93.43 F1) but slowest (12 min per 400 responses), whereas the GPTFuzzer classifier offered the best accuracy/speed trade-off (90.5% accuracy, 89.73 F1, 15s, lowest FPR at 3.92%).
core_contribution: EasyJailbreak is a unified, modular software framework for constructing and evaluating jailbreak attacks against LLMs. Its central abstraction decomposes any jailbreak method into four reusable components: a Selector (chooses the most promising candidate prompts from a pool, e.g. EXP3SelectPolicy, UCB, MCTS-Explore), a Mutator (transforms a rejected prompt to bypass safeguards, e.g. Translation into low-resource languages, Base64 encoding, gradient-based token mutation), a Constraint (filters inputs bound to fail, e.g. DeleteOffTopic, PerplexityConstraint), and an Evaluator (judges whether an attempt succeeded, e.g. ClassificationJudge, GenerativeJudge). By recombining these shared components the framework re-implements 11 published jailbreak 'recipes' (ReNeLLM, GPTFUZZER, ICA, AutoDAN, PAIR, JailBroken, Cipher, DeepInception, MultiLingual, GCG, TAP, plus CodeChameleon) under one interface, enabling — for the first time, per the authors — apples-to-apples benchmarking of these methods on the same data and victim models. The paper's own contribution is the framework, the component taxonomy, and a benchmark study; the individual attack methods themselves (GCG, PAIR, AutoDAN, etc.) are prior work by others that the framework wraps, not novel attacks introduced here.
threat_model: Adversary: a user (black-box or, for gradient methods like GCG, white-box) attempting to circumvent an aligned LLM's safety training to elicit prohibited/harmful outputs (e.g. instructions for weapons or poisons). The framework operationalizes attacker capability by iteratively mutating a malicious 'query' seeded with a jailbreak template, querying the target model, and re-evaluating. Its intended use, however, is defensive red-teaming: a benchmarking tool to identify and mitigate LLM vulnerabilities. The authors explicitly acknowledge the dual-use nature and include an ethics statement advocating responsible disclosure.
limitations: The paper is largely a systems/benchmark contribution and does not propose new attacks or defenses. Internal inconsistency in the count of supported methods (abstract/framework say 11, feature list says 12) and in the headline number (60% vs the 63% actually in Table 2). ASR is judged by an LLM (GPT-4-turbo) rather than humans in the main benchmark, so results inherit the judge's error (the evaluator study shows GPT4-turbo itself has a 7.35% FPR). Evaluation is confined to AdvBench and English-centric harmful queries; MultiLingual results are confounded by target models' language coverage (Llama2's weak multilingual ability lowers its MultiLingual ASR rather than reflecting stronger alignment). Only ~7B–13B open models plus two GPT versions were tested; larger models (e.g. Llama2-70B) are left to future work. Prompt-optimization methods are extremely expensive (GCG-class runs required ~764 hrs on Llama2-7B, ~804 hrs on Llama2-13B), limiting scale.

datasets_used: AdvBench (Zou et al., 2023) — harmful-behavior queries used as the attack benchmark
400 human-labeled model responses — used in the evaluator-comparison study (Table 3)
quantitative_results: Average breach probability across 10 models: 63% (Table 2; abstract/conclusion round to ~60%)
Closed-source average ASR 45% vs open-source average ASR 66%
Highest-ASR model: Mistral-7B 88%; lowest-ASR model: Llama2-7B-chat 31%; Vicuna13B 83%, ChatGLM3 77%, Vicuna7B 77%, Qwen-7B 74%, Intern7B 71%, Llama2-13B 37%
Per-method average ASR: CodeChameleon 80%, JailBroken 76%, MultiLingual 76%, AutoDAN 75%, Cipher 74%, ReNeLLM 70%, GPTFUZZER 65%, PAIR 63%, GCG 47%, DeepInception 32%, ICA 32%
Example cell values: JailBroken hits GPT-3.5-Turbo 100%; GCG hits GPT-4 0%; AutoDAN hits Vicuna7B 100%

Evaluator comparison (Table 3): GPT4-turbo 93.50% acc / 94.38% TPR / 7.35% FPR / 93.43 F1 / 12 min; GPTFuzzer Classifier 90.50% acc / 3.92% FPR / 89.73 F1 / 15s; ChatGPT 85.50% acc / 85.28 F1 / 3 min; Llama-Guard-7B 79.75% acc / 75.68 F1 / 3 min 30s; Rule Match 66.75% acc / 40.20% FPR / 68.56 F1 / 1s

Efficiency (Llama2, Appendix B/Fig.4): prompt-optimization methods dominated cost — total ~764.4 hrs (7B) and ~803.9 hrs (13B); AutoDAN alone ~476 hrs (7B) / ~502 hrs (13B); Cipher ASR on Llama2 61% (7B) and 90% (13B)
baselines_compared: 11 wrapped jailbreak recipes benchmarked against each other: ReNeLLM, GPTFUZZER, ICA, AutoDAN, PAIR, JailBroken, Cipher, DeepInception, MultiLingual, GCG, CodeChameleon (TAP also implemented; component chart in Table 1)
5 evaluator/judge methods compared: Rule Match, GPTFuzzer Classifier, Llama-Guard-7B, ChatGPT, GPT4-turbo
10 victim LLMs: GPT-4-0613, GPT-3.5-Turbo, Llama2-7B-chat, Llama2-13B-chat, Vicuna-7B-v1.5, Vicuna-13B-v1.5, Qwen-7B-chat, InternLM-chat-7B, ChatGLM3, Mistral-7B-v0.1
